1→# Component Boundaries -- MCP Tool Registry (#178)
     2→
     3→## Module Structure
     4→
     5→All new modules live under `app/src/server/` following existing conventions. No new top-level directories.
     6→
     7→```
     8→app/src/server/
     9→  proxy/
    10→    anthropic-proxy-route.ts   # MODIFIED: steps 7.5 + 8.5 added
    11→    tool-resolver.ts           # NEW: identity -> effective toolset query + cache
    12→    tool-injector.ts           # NEW: merge Brain tools into request tools[]
    13→    tool-router.ts             # NEW: classify + dispatch tool_calls
    14→    tool-executor.ts           # NEW: Brain-native + integration execution
    15→    credential-resolver.ts     # NEW: provider -> account -> decrypt -> inject auth
    16→    tool-trace-writer.ts       # NEW: tool_call-specific trace creation
    17→    trace-writer.ts            # EXISTING: no change (tool traces use separate writer)
    18→  tool-registry/
    19→    routes.ts                  # NEW: API routes for tool CRUD, grants, providers, accounts
    20→    queries.ts                 # NEW: SurrealDB queries for mcp_tool, can_use, governs_tool
    21→    provider-queries.ts        # NEW: credential_provider CRUD queries
    22→    account-queries.ts         # NEW: connected_account CRUD + OAuth callback queries
    23→    encryption.ts              # NEW: AES-256-GCM encrypt/decrypt adapter
    24→    oauth-flow.ts              # NEW: OAuth2 authorization URL builder + token exchange
    25→    mcp-discovery.ts           # NEW: MCP server connection + tools/list sync
    26→    types.ts                   # NEW: shared algebraic data types
    27→  runtime/
    28→    config.ts                  # MODIFIED: add toolEncryptionKey + toolResolutionCacheTtlMs
    29→    types.ts                   # NO CHANGE (ServerDependencies unchanged)
    30→```
    31→
    32→## Dependency Graph
    33→
    34→```
    35→proxy/anthropic-proxy-route.ts
    36→  |-- proxy/tool-resolver.ts        (resolves effective toolset)
    37→  |     |-- tool-registry/queries.ts  (can_use + mcp_tool queries)
    38→  |     '-- tool-registry/types.ts
    39→  |-- proxy/tool-injector.ts         (merges tools into request)
    40→  |     '-- tool-registry/types.ts
    41→  |-- proxy/tool-router.ts           (classifies tool calls)
    42→  |     |-- proxy/tool-executor.ts    (executes tool calls)
    43→  |     |     |-- proxy/credential-resolver.ts
    44→  |     |     |     |-- tool-registry/account-queries.ts
    45→  |     |     |     '-- tool-registry/encryption.ts
    46→  |     |     |-- proxy/tool-trace-writer.ts
    47→  |     |     |     '-- proxy/trace-writer.ts (reuses retry + edge patterns)
    48→  |     |     '-- chat/tools/*.ts     (Brain-native tool implementations)
    49→  |     '-- tool-registry/types.ts
    50→  '-- (existing: proxy-auth, context-injector, policy-evaluator, trace-writer)
    51→
    52→tool-registry/routes.ts
    53→  |-- tool-registry/queries.ts
    54→  |-- tool-registry/provider-queries.ts
    55→  |-- tool-registry/account-queries.ts
    56→  |-- tool-registry/encryption.ts
    57→  |-- tool-registry/oauth-flow.ts
    58→  |-- tool-registry/mcp-discovery.ts
    59→  '-- tool-registry/types.ts
    60→```
    61→
    62→## Component Responsibilities
    63→
    64→### proxy/tool-resolver.ts
    65→**Responsibility**: Given an identity ID, resolve the effective toolset (union of direct grants and skill-derived tools, deduplicated).
    66→
    67→**Port signature** (function-signature port, FP style):
    68→- Input: identity record ID, workspace record ID
    69→- Output: array of resolved tool definitions (name, description, input_schema, provider reference, risk_level)
    70→- Side effects: SurrealDB read (via injected query function), cache read/write
    71→
    72→**Caching**: In-memory `Map<identityId, { tools: ResolvedTool[], populatedAt: number }>` with configurable TTL (default 60s). Same pattern as `WorkspaceCache` in proxy route.
    73→
    74→**Query strategy**: Single SurrealDB round-trip:
    75→```
    76→SELECT mcp_tool.* FROM can_use WHERE in = $identity AND out.status = 'active' AND out.workspace = $workspace;
    77→```
    78→(Future: `UNION SELECT ... FROM possesses WHERE in = $identity -> skill_requires -> mcp_tool` when #177 ships)
    79→
    80→### proxy/tool-injector.ts
    81→**Responsibility**: Merge resolved Brain-managed tool definitions into the LLM request `tools[]` parameter. Additive only -- never modify or remove runtime tools.
    82→
    83→**Pure function**: Takes parsed request body + resolved tools, returns modified body with extended tools array. No side effects.
    84→
    85→**Deduplication**: If a runtime tool name collides with a Brain-managed tool name, the runtime tool takes precedence (Brain tool is skipped). This prevents conflicts when an agent runtime already provides a tool that Brain also manages.
    86→
    87→**Format**: Converts `mcp_tool` records to Anthropic Messages API tool format:
    88→```
    89→{ name, description, input_schema: { type: "object", properties: ..., required: ... } }
    90→```
    91→
    92→### proxy/tool-router.ts
    93→**Responsibility**: Given tool_use blocks from LLM response, classify each as brain-native, integration, or unknown, and dispatch to the appropriate executor.
    94→
    95→**Pure classification function**: Takes tool_call name, looks up in resolved toolset. Returns discriminated union:
    96→- `{ kind: "brain_native", tool: McpTool }` -- no provider, execute graph query
    97→- `{ kind: "integration", tool: McpTool, provider: CredentialProvider }` -- has provider, needs credentials
    98→- `{ kind: "unknown" }` -- not in registry, pass through
    99→
   100→### proxy/tool-executor.ts
   101→**Responsibility**: Execute tool calls by kind. Orchestrates Brain-native execution (graph queries) and integration execution (credential resolution + HTTP + sanitization).
   102→
   103→**Brain-native execution**: Reuses existing chat tool implementations from `chat/tools/*.ts`. These tools accept a context object and return results. The executor adapts the tool_call arguments into the expected format and returns the result as a tool_result message.
   104→
   105→**Integration execution**: Calls credential-resolver to get auth headers, makes HTTP request to the integration API endpoint, sanitizes response (strips auth headers and credential-bearing fields), returns sanitized result.
   106→
   107→**Error handling**: All execution errors are returned as tool_result error messages to the LLM (not thrown). The LLM can then decide how to proceed. Pattern: `{ type: "tool_result", tool_use_id, is_error: true, content: "..." }`.
   108→
   109→### proxy/credential-resolver.ts
   110→**Responsibility**: Given an mcp_tool with a provider reference and an identity, resolve the connected_account and return decrypted credentials ready for HTTP injection.
   111→
   112→**Pure core + effect shell**:
   113→- Pure: credential state classification (active/expired/missing/revoked), auth header construction by method
   114→- Effect: SurrealDB query for connected_account, decryption via encryption adapter, OAuth2 token refresh HTTP call
   115→
   116→**Token refresh logic** (OAuth2 only):
   117→1. Check `token_expires_at` against current time
   118→2. If expired and `refresh_token` exists: call `credential_provider.token_url` with refresh grant
   119→3. On success: update `connected_account` with new tokens, return fresh credential
   120→4. On failure: mark `connected_account.status = "expired"`, return error result
   121→
   122→**Result type** (discriminated union):
   123→- `{ kind: "ready", headers: Record<string, string> }` -- credential resolved, auth headers built
   124→- `{ kind: "not_connected" }` -- no connected_account for identity + provider
   125→- `{ kind: "expired" }` -- token expired and refresh failed
   126→- `{ kind: "revoked" }` -- account revoked
   127→
   128→### proxy/tool-trace-writer.ts
   129→**Responsibility**: Create `trace` records with `type: "tool_call"` for every Brain-managed tool execution.
   130→
   131→**Extends existing pattern**: Follows `trace-writer.ts` conventions (retry, async, inflight tracking). Adds tool-specific fields:
   132→- `tool_name`: the mcp_tool name
   133→- `outcome`: `"success" | "error" | "denied" | "rate_limited"`
   134→- `input`: tool_call arguments (sanitized)
   135→- `output`: tool result (sanitized, no credentials)
   136→
   137→### tool-registry/types.ts
   138→**Responsibility**: Shared algebraic data types for the tool registry domain.
   139→
   140→Key types:
   141→- `McpTool` -- domain representation of an mcp_tool record
   142→- `CredentialProvider` -- domain representation of a credential_provider record
   143→- `ConnectedAccount` -- domain representation (credential fields always encrypted at this layer)
   144→- `ResolvedTool` -- tool ready for injection (name, description, input_schema)
   145→- `ToolCallRoute` -- discriminated union for routing (brain_native | integration | unknown)
   146→- `CredentialResult` -- discriminated union for credential resolution outcome
   147→- `ToolExecutionOutcome` -- discriminated union for execution result
   148→
   149→### tool-registry/encryption.ts
   150→**Responsibility**: AES-256-GCM encrypt/decrypt for credential fields. Effect boundary adapter.
   151→
   152→**Interface**: Two pure-looking functions (encrypt and decrypt) that take plaintext/ciphertext + key and return ciphertext/plaintext. The key comes from `ServerConfig.toolEncryptionKey`.
   153→
   154→**IV handling**: Random 12-byte IV prepended to ciphertext. Each encryption produces a unique IV. Stored as base64 in SurrealDB string fields.
   155→
   156→### tool-registry/oauth-flow.ts
   157→**Responsibility**: Build OAuth2 authorization URLs and exchange authorization codes for tokens.
   158→
   159→**Pure functions**:
   160→- `buildAuthorizationUrl`: credential_provider fields -> URL with query params (client_id, redirect_uri, scope, state)
   161→- `parseOAuthCallback`: callback query params -> { code, state } or error
   162→
   163→**Effect functions**:
   164→- `exchangeCodeForTokens`: POST to token_url with code + client_secret -> { access_token, refresh_token, expires_in }
   165→- `refreshAccessToken`: POST to token_url with refresh_token -> { access_token, expires_in }
   166→
   167→### tool-registry/routes.ts
   168→**Responsibility**: REST API endpoints for tool registry management. All endpoints use existing DPoP authentication middleware.
   169→
   170→**Endpoints** (mounted under `/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/tools/`):
   171→- `GET /` -- list mcp_tools (filterable by toolkit, status, risk_level)
   172→- `POST /` -- create mcp_tool (admin only)
   173→- `PUT /:id` -- update mcp_tool (admin only)
   174→- `DELETE /:id` -- disable mcp_tool (admin only, sets status=disabled)
   175→- `GET /:id/grants` -- list can_use edges for a tool
   176→- `POST /:id/grants` -- create can_use edge (admin only)
   177→- `DELETE /:id/grants/:identityId` -- remove can_use edge (admin only)
   178→
   179→**Provider endpoints** (`/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/providers/`):
   180→- `GET /` -- list credential_providers
   181→- `POST /` -- register credential_provider (admin only)
   182→- `PUT /:id` -- update credential_provider (admin only)
   183→- `DELETE /:id` -- delete credential_provider (admin only)
   184→
   185→**Account endpoints** (`/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/accounts/`):
   186→- `GET /` -- list connected_accounts for current identity
   187→- `POST /connect/:providerId` -- initiate connection (OAuth2: returns redirect URL; static: accepts credentials)
   188→- `GET /callback` -- OAuth2 callback handler
   189→- `DELETE /:id` -- revoke connected_account
   190→
   191→**Discovery endpoints** (`/api/workspaces/:workspaceId/tools/discover`):
   192→- `POST /` -- connect MCP server + discover tools
   193→
   194→## Walking Skeleton Phase Mapping
   195→
   196→| Phase | Components Created/Modified | Independently Deployable |
   197→|---|---|---|
   198→| 1. US-3: Schema + grants | `tool-registry/types.ts`, `queries.ts`, `routes.ts` (partial: tool CRUD + grants) | Yes -- schema + API only, no proxy changes |
   199→| 2. US-5: Tool injection | `proxy/tool-resolver.ts`, `proxy/tool-injector.ts`, proxy route step 7.5 | Yes -- tools injected but not intercepted yet |
   200→| 3. US-6a: Brain-native routing | `proxy/tool-router.ts`, `proxy/tool-executor.ts` (brain-native path), proxy route step 8.5 | Yes -- brain-native tools work end-to-end |
   201→| 4. US-9: Tracing | `proxy/tool-trace-writer.ts` | Yes -- trace records written for tool executions |
   202→| 5-8: Credentials + integration | `encryption.ts`, `provider-queries.ts`, `account-queries.ts`, `oauth-flow.ts`, `credential-resolver.ts`, tool-executor integration path | Incremental -- each US adds a path |
   203→| 9. US-8: Governance | Policy evaluator extension for `governs_tool` | Yes -- layers onto existing policy engine |
   204→| 10. US-2: MCP discovery | `mcp-discovery.ts` | Yes -- admin-initiated, no proxy changes |
   205→

1→# Requirements — MCP Tool Registry (#178)
     2→
     3→## Functional Requirements
     4→
     5→### FR-1: Tool Registry Schema
     6→The system must persist MCP tool definitions as `mcp_tool` graph nodes with name, toolkit, description, input_schema, output_schema (optional, from MCP `tools/list` `outputSchema`), provider reference, risk_level, workspace scope, and status.
     7→
     8→### FR-2: Direct Tool Authorization
     9→The system must support `can_use` relation edges from identity to mcp_tool, enabling per-agent tool grants with optional rate limits (max_calls_per_hour).
    10→
    11→### FR-3: Tool Resolution
    12→The system must resolve an identity's effective toolset as the union of direct grants (`can_use`) and skill-derived tools (`possesses` -> `skill_requires`), with deduplication.
    13→
    14→### FR-4: Proxy Tool Injection
    15→The LLM proxy must inject Brain-managed tool definitions into the `tools[]` parameter of forwarded LLM requests, additively alongside runtime-provided tools.
    16→
    17→### FR-5: Proxy Tool Call Interception
    18→The proxy must intercept `tool_calls` in LLM responses and route them:
    19→- **Brain-native**: execute graph query directly
    20→- **Integration**: resolve credentials, execute, sanitize
    21→- **Unknown**: pass through to runtime
    22→
    23→### FR-6: Credential Provider Registration
    24→Workspace admins must be able to register credential providers with an auth method (`oauth2`, `api_key`, `bearer`, `basic`). For OAuth2: client credentials, authorization/token URLs, and scopes. For static methods: just a provider name (credentials are per-account). Secrets must be encrypted at rest.
    25→
    26→### FR-7: Account Connection (Multi-Method)
    27→Users must be able to connect accounts to providers via the provider's auth method:
    28→- **OAuth2**: standard authorization code flow (redirect → consent → callback → token exchange)
    29→- **API key / bearer / basic**: direct credential entry via form
    30→
    31→All credentials must be encrypted at rest.
    32→
    33→### FR-8: Credential Resolution
    34→The proxy must resolve credentials at tool call time by: mcp_tool.provider -> credential_provider -> connected_account (for identity + provider) -> inject credential by auth_method:
    35→- `oauth2`: `Authorization: Bearer {access_token}`
    36→- `api_key`: provider-specific header (e.g. `X-API-Key`)
    37→- `bearer`: `Authorization: Bearer {access_token}`
    38→- `basic`: `Authorization: Basic {base64(username:password)}`
    39→
    40→### FR-9: Token Refresh (OAuth2)
    41→For OAuth2 providers, the proxy must detect expired access tokens (via token_expires_at) and refresh them using the refresh_token before tool execution. Failed refresh must mark the connected_account as expired.
    42→
    43→### FR-10: Tool Governance
    44→Policies must be attachable to tools via `governs_tool` relation edges with conditions. The proxy must evaluate these before executing tool calls.
    45→
    46→### FR-11: MCP Server Discovery
    47→The system must support connecting to external MCP servers, calling `tools/list` to discover available tools, and creating `mcp_tool` records from the response.
    48→
    49→### FR-12: Tool Change Notifications
    50→The system must subscribe to `notifications/tools/list_changed` from connected MCP servers and refresh the tool registry accordingly.
    51→
    52→### FR-13: Trace Logging
    53→Every tool call execution (success, failure, denial, rate limit) must produce a trace record with tool_name, duration, outcome, and identity.
    54→
    55→## Non-Functional Requirements
    56→
    57→### NFR-1: Security
    58→- Credentials (client_secret, access_token, refresh_token, api_key, basic_password) must never appear in LLM context, agent logs, or API responses
    59→- All credential fields encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM
    60→- Tool results from integration calls must be sanitized (strip auth headers, tokens)
    61→
    62→### NFR-2: Latency
    63→- Tool resolution and injection must add < 50ms to proxy request processing
    64→- Credential resolution and token refresh must add < 200ms to tool execution
    65→- Tool resolution results should be cached per identity with short TTL
    66→
    67→### NFR-3: Compatibility
    68→- Tool injection must be additive — runtime tools in the request must not be modified or removed
    69→- Must work with any LLM provider that supports the Anthropic Messages API tool format
    70→- Must work with any agent runtime routing through Brain's proxy
    71→
    72→### NFR-4: Scalability
    73→- Tool resolution queries must be efficient for workspaces with 100+ tools and 50+ identities
    74→- Connected account lookups must be indexed by (identity, provider)
    75→
    76→### NFR-5: Auditability
    77→- Full provenance chain: tool_call -> mcp_tool -> credential_provider -> connected_account -> trace
    78→- All tool governance decisions (grant, deny, rate limit) must be traceable
    79→

1→# Data Models -- MCP Tool Registry (#178)
     2→
     3→## SurrealDB Schema
     4→
     5→### New Tables
     6→
     7→```sql
     8→-- Tool definition (graph node)
     9→DEFINE TABLE mcp_tool SCHEMAFULL;
    10→DEFINE FIELD name ON mcp_tool TYPE string;
    11→DEFINE FIELD toolkit ON mcp_tool TYPE string;
    12→DEFINE FIELD description ON mcp_tool TYPE string;
    13→DEFINE FIELD input_schema ON mcp_tool TYPE object FLEXIBLE;
    14→DEFINE FIELD output_schema ON mcp_tool TYPE option<object> FLEXIBLE;
    15→DEFINE FIELD provider ON mcp_tool TYPE option<record<credential_provider>>;
    16→DEFINE FIELD risk_level ON mcp_tool TYPE string
    17→  ASSERT $value IN ["low", "medium", "high", "critical"];
    18→DEFINE FIELD workspace ON mcp_tool TYPE record<workspace>;
    19→DEFINE FIELD status ON mcp_tool TYPE string
    20→  ASSERT $value IN ["active", "disabled"];
    21→DEFINE FIELD created_at ON mcp_tool TYPE datetime DEFAULT time::now();
    22→
    23→-- Indexes
    24→DEFINE INDEX mcp_tool_workspace ON mcp_tool FIELDS workspace;
    25→DEFINE INDEX mcp_tool_workspace_name ON mcp_tool FIELDS workspace, name;
    26→DEFINE INDEX mcp_tool_workspace_toolkit ON mcp_tool FIELDS workspace, toolkit;
    27→DEFINE INDEX mcp_tool_workspace_status ON mcp_tool FIELDS workspace, status;
    28→
    29→-- Tool authorization (relation edge: identity -> mcp_tool)
    30→DEFINE TABLE can_use TYPE RELATION IN identity OUT mcp_tool SCHEMAFULL;
    31→DEFINE FIELD granted_at ON can_use TYPE datetime DEFAULT time::now();
    32→DEFINE FIELD max_calls_per_hour ON can_use TYPE option<int>;
    33→
    34→DEFINE INDEX can_use_identity ON can_use FIELDS in;
    35→DEFINE INDEX can_use_tool ON can_use FIELDS out;
    36→
    37→-- Tool governance (relation edge: policy -> mcp_tool)
    38→DEFINE TABLE governs_tool TYPE RELATION IN policy OUT mcp_tool SCHEMAFULL;
    39→DEFINE FIELD conditions ON governs_tool TYPE option<string>;
    40→DEFINE FIELD max_per_call ON governs_tool TYPE option<float>;
    41→DEFINE FIELD max_per_day ON governs_tool TYPE option<float>;
    42→
    43→DEFINE INDEX governs_tool_tool ON governs_tool FIELDS out;
    44→
    45→-- Credential provider (graph node)
    46→DEFINE TABLE credential_provider SCHEMAFULL;
    47→DEFINE FIELD name ON credential_provider TYPE string;
    48→DEFINE FIELD display_name ON credential_provider TYPE string;
    49→DEFINE FIELD auth_method ON credential_provider TYPE string
    50→  ASSERT $value IN ["oauth2", "api_key", "bearer", "basic"];
    51→DEFINE FIELD authorization_url ON credential_provider TYPE option<string>;
    52→DEFINE FIELD token_url ON credential_provider TYPE option<string>;
    53→DEFINE FIELD client_id ON credential_provider TYPE option<string>;
    54→DEFINE FIELD client_secret_encrypted ON credential_provider TYPE option<string>;
    55→DEFINE FIELD scopes ON credential_provider TYPE option<array<string>>;
    56→DEFINE FIELD workspace ON credential_provider TYPE record<workspace>;
    57→DEFINE FIELD created_at ON credential_provider TYPE datetime DEFAULT time::now();
    58→
    59→DEFINE INDEX credential_provider_workspace ON credential_provider FIELDS workspace;
    60→DEFINE INDEX credential_provider_workspace_name ON credential_provider FIELDS workspace, name;
    61→
    62→-- Connected account (graph node)
    63→DEFINE TABLE connected_account SCHEMAFULL;
    64→DEFINE FIELD identity ON connected_account TYPE record<identity>;
    65→DEFINE FIELD provider ON connected_account TYPE record<credential_provider>;
    66→DEFINE FIELD access_token_encrypted ON connected_account TYPE option<string>;
    67→DEFINE FIELD refresh_token_encrypted ON connected_account TYPE option<string>;
    68→DEFINE FIELD token_expires_at ON connected_account TYPE option<datetime>;
    69→DEFINE FIELD api_key_encrypted ON connected_account TYPE option<string>;
    70→DEFINE FIELD basic_username ON connected_account TYPE option<string>;
    71→DEFINE FIELD basic_password_encrypted ON connected_account TYPE option<string>;
    72→DEFINE FIELD scopes ON connected_account TYPE option<array<string>>;
    73→DEFINE FIELD status ON connected_account TYPE string
    74→  ASSERT $value IN ["active", "expired", "revoked"];
    75→DEFINE FIELD connected_at ON connected_account TYPE datetime DEFAULT time::now();
    76→DEFINE FIELD workspace ON connected_account TYPE record<workspace>;
    77→
    78→DEFINE INDEX connected_account_identity_provider ON connected_account FIELDS identity, provider;
    79→DEFINE INDEX connected_account_workspace ON connected_account FIELDS workspace;
    80→DEFINE INDEX connected_account_identity ON connected_account FIELDS identity;
    81→```
    82→
    83→### Schema Design Decisions
    84→
    85→**Encrypted field naming**: Fields storing encrypted values use `_encrypted` suffix (e.g., `client_secret_encrypted` instead of `client_secret`). This makes it unambiguous that the stored value is ciphertext and prevents accidental plaintext reads.
    86→
    87→**No `client_secret` plaintext field**: The issue schema had `client_secret` as a plain string. This design stores ONLY the encrypted form. The original issue schema from #178 is treated as a logical model; the physical schema enforces encryption.
    88→
    89→**`option<string>` for encrypted fields**: Encrypted fields are `option<string>` because not all auth methods use all fields. An `api_key` provider has no `access_token_encrypted`. Omitted fields follow the project convention (no `null`, use omission).
    90→
    91→**Composite index on `connected_account(identity, provider)`**: This is the primary lookup path during credential resolution. The proxy needs to find "the connected_account for this identity and this provider" in a single indexed query. Per NFR-4, this must be efficient.
    92→
    93→**No UNIQUE index on `connected_account(identity, provider)`**: Per SurrealDB v3.0.4 bug (CLAUDE.md), UNIQUE indexes on optional record fields silently return empty results. Application-level uniqueness enforcement used instead (check-then-create in a transaction).
    94→
    95→## Query Patterns
    96→
    97→### Tool Resolution (Hot Path -- every proxy request)
    98→
    99→```sql
   100→-- Single query: resolve effective toolset for identity
   101→-- Returns active tools the identity can use in this workspace
   102→SELECT
   103→  out.id AS tool_id,
   104→  out.name AS name,
   105→  out.toolkit AS toolkit,
   106→  out.description AS description,
   107→  out.input_schema AS input_schema,
   108→  out.provider AS provider,
   109→  out.risk_level AS risk_level,
   110→  max_calls_per_hour
   111→FROM can_use
   112→WHERE in = $identity
   113→  AND out.status = 'active'
   114→  AND out.workspace = $workspace;
   115→```
   116→
   117→**Performance**: Uses `can_use_identity` index for the `in` filter. The `out.*` field access follows the relation to `mcp_tool` inline (SurrealDB resolves relation targets automatically). Expected < 5ms for workspaces with < 100 tools.
   118→
   119→**Future skill-derived resolution** (#177):
   120→```sql
   121→-- When skill tables exist, UNION with:
   122→SELECT out.id AS tool_id, out.name, ... FROM skill_requires
   123→WHERE in IN (SELECT out FROM possesses WHERE in = $identity)
   124→  AND out.status = 'active'
   125→  AND out.workspace = $workspace;
   126→```
   127→
   128→### Credential Resolution (Per tool call)
   129→
   130→```sql
   131→-- Resolve connected_account for identity + provider
   132→SELECT *
   133→FROM connected_account
   134→WHERE identity = $identity
   135→  AND provider = $provider
   136→  AND status = 'active'
   137→LIMIT 1;
   138→```
   139→
   140→**Performance**: Uses `connected_account_identity_provider` composite index. Expected < 2ms.
   141→
   142→### Rate Limit Check (Per tool call)
   143→
   144→```sql
   145→-- Count tool calls in the last hour for rate limiting
   146→SELECT count() AS call_count
   147→FROM trace
   148→WHERE type = 'tool_call'
   149→  AND tool_name = $tool_name
   150→  AND actor = $identity
   151→  AND created_at > time::now() - 1h;
   152→```
   153→
   154→**Performance**: Uses `trace_workspace` index + filter. For high-volume tools, consider in-memory sliding window (same pattern as `rate-limiter.ts` in proxy).
   155→
   156→### Tool Governance Check (Per tool call)
   157→
   158→```sql
   159→-- Check if any policy governs this tool
   160→SELECT in AS policy_id, conditions, max_per_call, max_per_day
   161→FROM governs_tool
   162→WHERE out = $tool;
   163→```
   164→
   165→**Performance**: Uses `governs_tool_tool` index. Expected < 2ms.
   166→
   167→### Provider Listing (Admin UI)
   168→
   169→```sql
   170→SELECT * FROM credential_provider WHERE workspace = $workspace ORDER BY created_at DESC;
   171→```
   172→
   173→### Tool Listing (Admin UI)
   174→
   175→```sql
   176→SELECT *, (SELECT count() FROM can_use WHERE out = $parent.id) AS grant_count
   177→FROM mcp_tool
   178→WHERE workspace = $workspace AND status = $status
   179→ORDER BY toolkit, name;
   180→```
   181→
   182→## Encryption Strategy
   183→
   184→### Algorithm
   185→- **AES-256-GCM** (authenticated encryption with associated data)
   186→- 256-bit key from `ServerConfig.toolEncryptionKey`
   187→- Random 12-byte IV per encryption operation
   188→- 16-byte authentication tag
   189→
   190→### Key Management
   191→- Key stored as environment variable, parsed into `ServerConfig` at startup
   192→- Key format: 32-byte hex string (64 characters) or base64-encoded 32 bytes
   193→- No key rotation in walking skeleton; key rotation is a future enhancement
   194→- Key absence: tool registry features that require encryption are disabled (fail-fast on provider registration)
   195→
   196→### Storage Format
   197→- Ciphertext stored as base64 string: `base64(IV || ciphertext || authTag)`
   198→- IV: first 12 bytes; auth tag: last 16 bytes; ciphertext: middle bytes
   199→- Parsing: split on known offsets after base64 decode
   200→
   201→### Encrypted Fields
   202→
   203→| Table | Field | Contains |
   204→|---|---|---|
   205→| `credential_provider` | `client_secret_encrypted` | OAuth2 client secret |
   206→| `connected_account` | `access_token_encrypted` | OAuth2 access token |
   207→| `connected_account` | `refresh_token_encrypted` | OAuth2 refresh token |
   208→| `connected_account` | `api_key_encrypted` | API key |
   209→| `connected_account` | `basic_password_encrypted` | Basic auth password |
   210→
   211→### Fields NOT Encrypted
   212→- `credential_provider.client_id` -- not a secret (public in OAuth2 spec)
   213→- `credential_provider.authorization_url`, `token_url` -- public URLs
   214→- `connected_account.basic_username` -- not sensitive (username portion of basic auth)
   215→- `connected_account.scopes` -- not sensitive (list of permission names)
   216→- `connected_account.token_expires_at` -- metadata, not a credential
   217→
   218→## Trace Schema Extension
   219→
   220→The existing `trace` table already supports `type: "tool_call"` and has `tool_name`, `input`, `output`, `duration_ms`, `actor`, `workspace` fields. No schema change required for basic tool tracing.
   221→
   222→**Additional fields for tool call traces** (stored in FLEXIBLE `input`/`output` fields):
   223→- `input.tool_arguments`: the tool_call arguments (sanitized)
   224→- `input.tool_kind`: `"brain_native" | "integration"`
   225→- `input.credential_provider_id`: provider reference (integration calls only)
   226→- `output.tool_result`: the execution result (sanitized, no credentials)
   227→- `output.outcome`: `"success" | "error" | "denied" | "rate_limited"`
   228→
   229→No migration needed -- `input` and `output` are already `option<object> FLEXIBLE`.
   230→
   231→## Migration Plan
   232→
   233→**Migration 0065**: Creates `mcp_tool`, `can_use`, `governs_tool`, `credential_provider`, `connected_account` tables with all indexes.
   234→
   235→**Phased migration** following walking skeleton order:
   236→1. Phase 1 (US-3): `mcp_tool` + `can_use` tables only
   237→2. Phase 5 (US-1): `credential_provider` table
   238→3. Phase 6 (US-4): `connected_account` table
   239→4. Phase 9 (US-8): `governs_tool` table
   240→
   241→Single migration file is preferred (tables with no data don't need incremental creation), but the walking skeleton can ship with a subset and add tables in later migrations if needed.
   242→
   243→## Data Flow Diagram
   244→
   245→```
   246→[Admin registers provider]
   247→  |
   248→  v
   249→credential_provider (encrypted client_secret)
   250→  |
   251→  v
   252→[Admin creates mcp_tool with provider reference]
   253→  |
   254→  v
   255→mcp_tool (workspace-scoped, toolkit-namespaced)
   256→  |
   257→  v
   258→[Admin grants access]
   259→  |
   260→  v
   261→can_use (identity -> mcp_tool edge)
   262→  |
   263→  v
   264→[User connects account]
   265→  |
   266→  v
   267→connected_account (identity -> credential_provider, encrypted tokens)
   268→  |
   269→  v
   270→[Proxy request arrives]
   271→  |
   272→  v
   273→Tool Resolution: identity -> can_use -> mcp_tool (cached 60s)
   274→  |
   275→  v
   276→Tool Injection: mcp_tool -> Anthropic tool format -> append to tools[]
   277→  |
   278→  v
   279→[LLM responds with tool_use]
   280→  |
   281→  v
   282→Tool Routing: tool_name -> mcp_tool lookup -> classify (brain-native | integration | unknown)
   283→  |
   284→  |-- brain-native: execute graph query directly
   285→  |-- integration: credential_provider -> connected_account -> decrypt -> inject auth -> HTTP -> sanitize
   286→  '-- unknown: pass through to runtime
   287→  |
   288→  v
   289→Trace: tool_call record with outcome + provenance
   290→```
   291→

1→# User Stories — MCP Tool Registry (#178)
     2→
     3→Each story traces to at least one JTBD job story.
     4→
     5→---
     6→
     7→## Epic: MCP Tool Registry
     8→
     9→### US-1: Register Credential Provider [J1]
    10→
    11→**As a** workspace admin
    12→**I want to** register a credential provider (OAuth2, API key, bearer, or basic auth)
    13→**So that** workspace members can connect their accounts and agents can use integration tools
    14→
    15→**Size**: M
    16→**Priority**: Must-have (Walking Skeleton prerequisite)
    17→
    18→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-1
    19→
    20→---
    21→
    22→### US-2: Discover Tools from MCP Server [J1]
    23→
    24→**As a** workspace admin
    25→**I want to** connect an MCP server and have Brain discover its tools automatically
    26→**So that** I don't have to manually define each tool's schema
    27→
    28→**Size**: L
    29→**Priority**: Should-have (manual tool creation is the walking skeleton path)
    30→
    31→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-2
    32→
    33→---
    34→
    35→### US-3: Grant Tool Access to Identity [J1, J3]
    36→
    37→**As a** workspace admin
    38→**I want to** grant specific tool access to agent identities via can_use edges
    39→**So that** I control which agents can use which tools
    40→
    41→**Size**: S
    42→**Priority**: Must-have (Walking Skeleton)
    43→
    44→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-3
    45→
    46→---
    47→
    48→### US-4: Connect Account [J2]
    49→
    50→**As a** workspace member
    51→**I want to** connect my account to a provider — via OAuth flow or by entering credentials directly
    52→**So that** agents can act with my identity without me sharing credentials outside Brain
    53→
    54→**Size**: L
    55→**Priority**: Must-have (required for credential brokerage)
    56→
    57→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-4
    58→
    59→---
    60→
    61→### US-5: Proxy Injects Tools into LLM Request [J3]
    62→
    63→**As an** agent routing through Brain's proxy
    64→**I want** Brain-managed tools injected into my LLM requests automatically
    65→**So that** I have integration capabilities without runtime-specific configuration
    66→
    67→**Size**: L
    68→**Priority**: Must-have (Walking Skeleton core)
    69→
    70→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-5
    71→
    72→---
    73→
    74→### US-6a: Proxy Routes Brain-Native Tool Calls [J3]
    75→
    76→**As an** agent
    77→**I want** the proxy to intercept tool calls for Brain-native tools (graph queries, context tools) and execute them directly
    78→**So that** I get seamless access to Brain's knowledge graph without runtime-specific wiring
    79→
    80→**Size**: L
    81→**Priority**: Must-have (Walking Skeleton core)
    82→
    83→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-6 (Brain-native and pass-through scenarios)
    84→
    85→---
    86→
    87→### US-6b: Proxy Routes Integration Tool Calls [J3, J4]
    88→
    89→**As an** agent
    90→**I want** the proxy to intercept tool calls for integration tools and execute them with brokered credentials
    91→**So that** I can use GitHub/Slack/Linear tools without holding any credentials
    92→
    93→**Size**: L
    94→**Priority**: Must-have (after US-7 credential brokerage)
    95→
    96→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-6 (integration scenario) + AC-7
    97→
    98→---
    99→
   100→### US-7: Credential Brokerage at Execution [J4]
   101→
   102→**As a** Brain proxy
   103→**I want to** resolve and inject credentials by auth method at tool call time
   104→**So that** agents never see raw tokens/keys and OAuth2 credentials are always fresh
   105→
   106→**Size**: L
   107→**Priority**: Must-have (required for integration tools)
   108→
   109→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-7
   110→
   111→---
   112→
   113→### US-8: Tool Governance via Policy [J1, J4]
   114→
   115→**As a** workspace admin
   116→**I want to** attach governance policies to tools
   117→**So that** high-risk tool calls require approval or are rate-limited
   118→
   119→**Size**: M
   120→**Priority**: Should-have
   121→
   122→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-8
   123→
   124→---
   125→
   126→### US-9: Tool Call Tracing [J4]
   127→
   128→**As a** workspace admin or auditor
   129→**I want** every tool call to produce a forensic trace record
   130→**So that** I can audit what agents did with integration tools
   131→
   132→**Size**: S
   133→**Priority**: Must-have (Walking Skeleton)
   134→
   135→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-9
   136→
   137→---
   138→
   139→### US-10: Revoke Connected Account [J2]
   140→
   141→**As a** workspace member
   142→**I want to** disconnect my account from a provider
   143→**So that** agents can no longer act with my credentials
   144→
   145→**Size**: S
   146→**Priority**: Must-have
   147→
   148→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-10
   149→
   150→---
   151→
   152→### US-11: Tool Registry UI [J1, J2]
   153→
   154→**As a** workspace admin or member
   155→**I want** a Tool Registry UI to browse tools, manage providers, connect accounts, and grant access
   156→**So that** I can govern the workspace's integration toolset without CLI or raw API calls
   157→
   158→**Size**: XL
   159→**Priority**: Must-have (final phase — backend-first, UI layers on top)
   160→
   161→**Sub-capabilities**:
   162→- **Browse tools**: list workspace `mcp_tool` records grouped by toolkit, filterable by status/risk_level, showing name, description, risk_level, provider, and grant count
   163→- **Provider management**: register/edit/delete `credential_provider` records — form adapts to auth_method (OAuth2 shows client_id/secret/URLs/scopes; api_key/bearer/basic shows only name/display_name)
   164→- **Account connection**: "Connect" button per provider — OAuth2 triggers redirect flow, static methods show credential entry form. Shows connection status (active/expired/revoked) with reconnect action
   165→- **Tool access management**: assign `can_use` edges from identities to tools, with optional max_calls_per_hour. View effective toolset per identity (direct + skill-derived)
   166→- **Tool governance**: attach `governs_tool` policy edges to tools with conditions and limits
   167→- **MCP server connection**: connect external MCP servers, trigger `tools/list` discovery, review discovered tools before import
   168→- **Connected accounts dashboard**: list connected accounts per identity with status, provider, scopes, connected_at. Revoke action deletes credentials
   169→
   170→**Acceptance Criteria**: See AC-11
   171→
   172→---
   173→
   174→## Walking Skeleton Stories (ordered)
   175→
   176→1. **US-3** — Grant tool access (schema + can_use)
   177→2. **US-5** — Proxy tool injection
   178→3. **US-6a** — Proxy routes Brain-native tool calls
   179→4. **US-9** — Tool call tracing
   180→5. **US-1** — Credential provider registration (all auth methods)
   181→6. **US-4** — Account connection (static credentials first, then OAuth2)
   182→7. **US-7** — Credential brokerage (static injection first, then OAuth2 with refresh)
   183→8. **US-6b** — Proxy routes integration tool calls
   184→9. **US-8** — Tool governance
   185→10. **US-2** — MCP server discovery
   186→11. **US-10** — Account revocation
   187→12. **US-11** — Tool Registry UI (browse, manage, connect, grant)
   188→

1→# Acceptance Criteria — MCP Tool Registry (#178)
     2→
     3→## AC-1: Register Credential Provider [US-1, FR-6]
     4→
     5→```gherkin
     6→Given a workspace admin identity
     7→When registering an OAuth2 provider with name, auth_method="oauth2", authorization_url, token_url, client_id, client_secret, scopes
     8→Then a credential_provider record is created in the workspace
     9→And client_secret is encrypted at rest (not stored plaintext)
    10→And the provider appears in workspace provider listings
    11→```
    12→
    13→```gherkin
    14→Given a workspace admin identity
    15→When registering an API key provider with name, auth_method="api_key"
    16→Then a credential_provider record is created with no OAuth-specific fields
    17→And the provider appears in workspace provider listings
    18→```
    19→
    20→```gherkin
    21→Given a credential_provider already exists with name "github" in the workspace
    22→When registering another provider with name "github"
    23→Then the system rejects with a duplicate name error
    24→```
    25→
    26→---
    27→
    28→## AC-2: Discover Tools from MCP Server [US-2, FR-11, FR-12]
    29→
    30→```gherkin
    31→Given a connected MCP server serving 5 tools via tools/list
    32→When Brain fetches the tool list
    33→Then 5 mcp_tool records are created with name, description, input_schema, and output_schema (when present) from the response
    34→And tools with destructiveHint=true get risk_level "high"
    35→And tools with readOnlyHint=true get risk_level "low"
    36→And tools without hints get risk_level "medium"
    37→```
    38→
    39→```gherkin
    40→Given a connected MCP server that supports listChanged notifications
    41→When the server pushes notifications/tools/list_changed
    42→Then Brain re-fetches tools/list
    43→And diffs against stored mcp_tool records
    44→And creates new tools, updates changed schemas, marks removed tools as disabled
    45→```
    46→
    47→---
    48→
    49→## AC-3: Grant Tool Access [US-3, FR-2]
    50→
    51→```gherkin
    52→Given mcp_tool "search_entities" and identity "agent-1"
    53→When admin creates a can_use edge
    54→Then the edge is persisted with granted_at timestamp
    55→And "agent-1" effective toolset includes "search_entities"
    56→```
    57→
    58→```gherkin
    59→Given a can_use edge with max_calls_per_hour=10
    60→When "agent-1" has made 10 calls in the current hour
    61→Then the 11th call returns rate limit error with reset time
    62→```
    63→
    64→---
    65→
    66→## AC-4: Connect Account [US-4, FR-7]
    67→
    68→### AC-4a: OAuth2 Connection
    69→
    70→```gherkin
    71→Given credential_provider "github" with auth_method="oauth2" in the workspace
    72→When user initiates connection
    73→Then Brain redirects to authorization_url with client_id, scopes, state, redirect_uri
    74→```
    75→
    76→```gherkin
    77→Given the provider redirects back with authorization_code
    78→When Brain exchanges the code at token_url
    79→Then a connected_account is created with encrypted access_token and refresh_token
    80→And status is "active"
    81→And scopes reflect what was granted
    82→```
    83→
    84→```gherkin
    85→Given the user denies consent at the provider
    86→When the provider redirects back with error
    87→Then no connected_account is created
    88→And user sees "Connection cancelled" message
    89→```
    90→
    91→### AC-4b: Static Credential Connection
    92→
    93→```gherkin
    94→Given credential_provider "internal-api" with auth_method="api_key" in the workspace
    95→When user submits their API key
    96→Then a connected_account is created with encrypted api_key
    97→And status is "active"
    98→```
    99→
   100→```gherkin
   101→Given credential_provider "legacy-service" with auth_method="basic" in the workspace
   102→When user submits username and password
   103→Then a connected_account is created with basic_username and encrypted basic_password
   104→And status is "active"
   105→```
   106→
   107→---
   108→
   109→## AC-5: Proxy Tool Injection [US-5, FR-3, FR-4]
   110→
   111→```gherkin
   112→Given identity "agent-1" with can_use edges to ["github.create_issue", "search_entities"]
   113→And the LLM request contains runtime tools ["read_file", "write_file"]
   114→When the proxy processes the request
   115→Then the forwarded request tools[] contains all 4 tools
   116→And runtime tools are unmodified (same schema, same position)
   117→And Brain-managed tools have correct name, description, input_schema from mcp_tool records
   118→```
   119→
   120→```gherkin
   121→Given identity "agent-1" possesses skill "code-review"
   122→And skill "code-review" requires tool "github.list_reviews" via skill_requires
   123→And identity "agent-1" also has direct can_use to "github.list_reviews"
   124→When the proxy resolves the effective toolset
   125→Then "github.list_reviews" appears exactly once (deduplicated)
   126→```
   127→
   128→```gherkin
   129→Given identity "new-agent" with no can_use edges and no skills
   130→When the proxy processes the request
   131→Then no tools are added to the request (only runtime tools remain)
   132→```
   133→
   134→---
   135→
   136→## AC-6: Proxy Tool Call Routing [US-6, FR-5]
   137→
   138→```gherkin
   139→Given the LLM returns tool_call for "search_entities" (Brain-native)
   140→When the proxy intercepts the tool call
   141→Then the proxy executes the graph query directly
   142→And returns the result to the LLM
   143→And writes a trace record
   144→```
   145→
   146→```gherkin
   147→Given the LLM returns tool_call for "github.create_issue" (integration)
   148→When the proxy intercepts the tool call
   149→Then the proxy routes to credential resolution (see AC-7)
   150→```
   151→
   152→```gherkin
   153→Given the LLM returns tool_call for "read_file" (unknown to Brain)
   154→When the proxy intercepts the tool call
   155→Then the proxy passes the tool call through to the runtime
   156→And does not write a trace record for it
   157→```
   158→
   159→---
   160→
   161→## AC-7: Credential Brokerage [US-7, FR-8, FR-9]
   162→
   163→### AC-7a: OAuth2 Credential Injection
   164→
   165→```gherkin
   166→Given mcp_tool "github.create_issue" with provider -> credential_provider "github" (auth_method="oauth2")
   167→And connected_account for identity + provider with valid access_token
   168→When the proxy executes the tool call
   169→Then the access_token is attached as Authorization: Bearer header
   170→And the API call is made to the tool's endpoint
   171→And credentials are stripped from the response before returning to LLM
   172→```
   173→
   174→```gherkin
   175→Given connected_account with expired access_token and valid refresh_token
   176→When the proxy attempts tool execution
   177→Then the proxy sends refresh request to credential_provider.token_url
   178→And updates connected_account with new access_token and token_expires_at
   179→And proceeds with execution using the new token
   180→```
   181→
   182→```gherkin
   183→Given connected_account with expired access_token and expired/invalid refresh_token
   184→When the proxy attempts tool execution
   185→Then connected_account.status is set to "expired"
   186→And the proxy returns error: "GitHub credentials expired. Please reconnect."
   187→And no API call is made
   188→```
   189→
   190→### AC-7b: Static Credential Injection
   191→
   192→```gherkin
   193→Given mcp_tool "internal.query" with provider -> credential_provider "internal-api" (auth_method="api_key")
   194→And connected_account for identity + provider with api_key
   195→When the proxy executes the tool call
   196→Then the api_key is attached as the provider-specific header
   197→And credentials are stripped from the response
   198→```
   199→
   200→```gherkin
   201→Given mcp_tool "legacy.fetch" with provider -> credential_provider "legacy-service" (auth_method="basic")
   202→And connected_account with basic_username and basic_password
   203→When the proxy executes the tool call
   204→Then Authorization: Basic {base64(username:password)} header is attached
   205→And credentials are stripped from the response
   206→```
   207→
   208→### AC-7c: Missing Account
   209→
   210→```gherkin
   211→Given no connected_account exists for the identity and provider
   212→When the proxy intercepts a tool call for that provider's tool
   213→Then the proxy returns error: "Provider account not connected."
   214→And no API call is made
   215→```
   216→
   217→---
   218→
   219→## AC-8: Tool Governance [US-8, FR-10]
   220→
   221→```gherkin
   222→Given policy "no-auto-merge" with governs_tool edge to "github.merge_pr"
   223→And governs_tool has condition "requires_human_approval"
   224→When the proxy intercepts tool_call for "github.merge_pr"
   225→Then the proxy evaluates the policy
   226→And returns error: "Tool call denied by policy: requires human approval"
   227→And writes trace with denial reason

   231→
   232→## AC-9: Tool Call Tracing [US-9, FR-13]
   233→
   234→```gherkin
   235→Given any Brain-managed tool call (native or integration)
   236→When the proxy executes the call
   237→Then a trace record is created with:
   238→  | field       | value                              |

   241→  | identity    | the calling identity               |
   242→  | workspace   | the workspace                      |
   243→  | duration_ms | execution time                     |
   244→  | outcome     | success / error / denied / rate_limited |
   245→```
   246→
   247→---
   248→
   249→## AC-10: Revoke Connected Account [US-10]
   250→
   251→```gherkin
   252→Given an active connected_account for identity "user-1" and provider "github"
   253→When "user-1" revokes the connection

   255→And subsequent tool calls for github tools return "account disconnected" error
   256→And all credential fields (access_token, refresh_token, api_key, basic_password) are deleted (not just marked)

1→# ADR-064: Proxy Tool Injection via Request Body Mutation
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→Proposed
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→The MCP Tool Registry (#178) needs to inject Brain-managed tool definitions into LLM requests. The proxy already mutates request bodies for context injection (ADR-046). We need to decide how tool definitions are delivered to the LLM.
     8→
     9→Three constraints shape this decision:
    10→1. Tools must appear in the Anthropic Messages API `tools[]` parameter for the LLM to use them
    11→2. Agent runtimes (Claude Code, Cursor) already provide their own tools in the request
    12→3. Tool injection must be additive -- runtime tools must not be modified or removed
    13→
    14→## Decision
    15→Inject Brain-managed tools by appending to the `tools[]` array in the parsed request body, following the same body mutation pattern as context injection (step 7 in the proxy pipeline). This becomes step 7.5, after context injection and before forwarding.
    16→
    17→Runtime tools occupy the front of the array (original positions preserved). Brain-managed tools are appended at the end. If a Brain-managed tool name collides with a runtime tool name, the Brain tool is skipped (runtime takes precedence).
    18→
    19→## Alternatives Considered
    20→
    21→### Alternative 1: Separate tools-only request header
    22→- **What**: Send Brain tools in a custom `X-Brain-Tools` header, rely on the LLM provider to merge them.
    23→- **Expected impact**: Zero body mutation.
    24→- **Why rejected**: Anthropic's Messages API ignores custom headers for tool definitions. The model only sees `tools[]` in the request body. Same rejection rationale as ADR-046 Alternative 2.
    25→
    26→### Alternative 2: MCP server-side tool provision
    27→- **What**: Brain exposes tools via its existing CLI MCP server (stdio), agents discover them via MCP protocol.
    28→- **Expected impact**: Standard MCP discovery, no proxy changes.
    29→- **Why rejected**: Only works for agent runtimes that support MCP (Claude Code). Does not work for Cursor, Codex, or any HTTP-only agent routing through the proxy. The proxy path is runtime-agnostic. Additionally, MCP tools don't carry credential brokerage -- the agent runtime would need to handle credentials, defeating the security model.
    30→
    31→### Alternative 3: Inject tools via system prompt description
    32→- **What**: Describe available tools in the system prompt `<brain-context>` block, relying on the LLM to "call" them via structured text output.
    33→- **Expected impact**: No tools[] mutation.
    34→- **Why rejected**: LLMs use structured tool_use blocks only when tools are in the `tools[]` parameter. System prompt descriptions produce unstructured text that the proxy cannot reliably intercept and route. Breaks the tool call interception pipeline.
    35→
    36→## Consequences
    37→- **Positive**: Runtime-agnostic -- any agent routing through the proxy gets tools injected
    38→- **Positive**: Follows established proxy body mutation pattern (ADR-046)
    39→- **Positive**: Additive injection preserves prompt cache for runtime tools (they're at the same array positions)
    40→- **Positive**: Deduplication prevents tool name collisions
    41→- **Negative**: Increases request body size proportional to number of injected tools (~200-500 bytes per tool definition)
    42→- **Negative**: Too many injected tools could confuse the LLM -- mitigated by token budget for tool definitions (same approach as context injection budget)
    43→

1→# ADR-065: TTL Cache for Tool Resolution
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→Proposed
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→Tool resolution (identity -> can_use edges -> mcp_tool records) runs on every proxy request. NFR-2 requires < 50ms overhead. The SurrealDB query itself is expected at < 5ms with indexes, but network round-trip and connection overhead add variance. The proxy already uses TTL caches for workspace validation (60s) and proxy token resolution (5min).
     8→
     9→We need a caching strategy that balances latency with freshness. Tool grants change infrequently (admin action), so staleness is acceptable within bounds.
    10→
    11→## Decision
    12→In-memory `Map<string, { tools: ResolvedTool[], populatedAt: number }>` keyed by identity ID, with a 60-second TTL. Same pattern as `WorkspaceCache` in the proxy route. TTL enforced at read time (check `populatedAt` against `Date.now()`). Stale entries pruned by the existing periodic pruning interval in the proxy handler factory.
    13→
    14→No event-driven invalidation. Cache entries expire naturally. An admin granting tool access takes effect within 60 seconds.
    15→
    16→## Alternatives Considered
    17→
    18→### Alternative 1: No cache (query every request)
    19→- **What**: Query SurrealDB on every proxy request for tool resolution.
    20→- **Expected impact**: Always fresh, 3-8ms per query.
    21→- **Why rejected**: Under load, adds unnecessary round-trips. The proxy already caches workspace validation and proxy tokens for the same reason. Consistency with existing patterns.
    22→
    23→### Alternative 2: SurrealDB LIVE SELECT for push invalidation
    24→- **What**: Subscribe to `can_use` table changes via LIVE SELECT, invalidate specific cache entries on mutation.
    25→- **Expected impact**: Near-instant invalidation, always fresh.
    26→- **Why rejected**: LIVE SELECT requires a persistent WebSocket connection per subscription (ADR-056). Adds operational complexity for a cache that serves admin-initiated changes (infrequent). The 60s staleness is acceptable for the use case -- tools are granted by admins, not in real-time loops. Over-engineering for the current scale.
    27→
    28→### Alternative 3: Redis/external cache
    29→- **What**: Use Redis or another external cache for distributed caching.
    30→- **Expected impact**: Shared cache across multiple server instances.
    31→- **Why rejected**: Brain runs as a single server instance (ADR-040). No multi-instance deployment exists. Adding Redis as a dependency for a simple TTL cache violates the simplest-solution principle and the OSS-minimalism constraint.
    32→
    33→## Consequences
    34→- **Positive**: Consistent with existing proxy caching patterns (workspace, token)
    35→- **Positive**: Zero external dependencies
    36→- **Positive**: < 1ms cache hit latency
    37→- **Positive**: Pruning integrated into existing periodic cleanup interval
    38→- **Negative**: Up to 60s staleness on tool grant changes (acceptable for admin-initiated operations)
    39→- **Negative**: Cache is per-process (not shared) -- acceptable for single-instance deployment
    40→

1→# ADR-066: AES-256-GCM for Credential Encryption at Rest
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→Proposed
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→NFR-1 requires all credential fields (client_secret, access_token, refresh_token, api_key, basic_password) encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM. Credentials must never appear in LLM context, agent logs, or API responses.
     8→
     9→The encryption must happen at the application layer (not database-level) because:
    10→1. SurrealDB does not offer transparent field-level encryption
    11→2. Credentials must be decrypted only at the execution boundary (credential-resolver), not on every DB read
    12→3. The encrypted form must be opaque to any SurrealDB query or admin console access
    13→
    14→## Decision
    15→Use Node.js/Bun native `crypto` module for AES-256-GCM encryption. Each encrypted field stored as a base64 string containing `IV || ciphertext || authTag`. The encryption key is a 256-bit key loaded from `ServerConfig.toolEncryptionKey` at startup.
    16→
    17→Encrypted field names use `_encrypted` suffix in the schema (e.g., `client_secret_encrypted`) to make ciphertext storage explicit and prevent accidental plaintext reads.
    18→
    19→Decryption happens only in `credential-resolver.ts` at the moment of tool call execution. No other code path decrypts credentials.
    20→
    21→## Alternatives Considered
    22→
    23→### Alternative 1: SurrealDB-level encryption (surrealkv encryption at rest)
    24→- **What**: Rely on SurrealKV's storage engine encryption for at-rest protection.
    25→- **Expected impact**: Zero application code for encryption.
    26→- **Why rejected**: SurrealKV encryption protects the storage files, not individual fields. Any SurrealDB query returns plaintext. Admin console access, query logs, and backup exports would contain raw credentials. Does not satisfy NFR-1's requirement that credentials never appear in query results.
    27→
    28→### Alternative 2: HashiCorp Vault for key management + encryption
    29→- **What**: Use Vault's Transit secrets engine for encrypt/decrypt operations.
    30→- **Expected impact**: Enterprise-grade key management with rotation, audit, and access policies.
    31→- **Why rejected**: Adds external service dependency (Vault) to a single-developer project. Operational complexity (Vault server, unsealing, token renewal) exceeds the current deployment model. The encryption key in `ServerConfig` is sufficient for the current threat model. Vault can be layered on later by replacing the encryption adapter.
    32→
    33→### Alternative 3: libsodium (sodium-native) for encryption
    34→- **What**: Use libsodium's `crypto_secretbox_easy` (XSalsa20-Poly1305) instead of AES-256-GCM.
    35→- **Expected impact**: Slightly simpler API, misuse-resistant.
    36→- **Why rejected**: Requires native addon (`sodium-native`), which adds compilation dependency. Bun's native `crypto` module supports AES-256-GCM natively with zero additional dependencies. AES-256-GCM is the industry standard for authenticated encryption and is NIST-approved.
    37→
    38→## Consequences
    39→- **Positive**: Zero external dependencies (Bun native crypto)
    40→- **Positive**: Authenticated encryption (GCM) prevents tampering
    41→- **Positive**: Unique IV per encryption prevents ciphertext analysis
    42→- **Positive**: `_encrypted` suffix makes ciphertext explicit in schema and code
    43→- **Positive**: Encryption adapter is swappable (Vault, KMS) without changing the rest of the system
    44→- **Negative**: Key rotation requires re-encryption of all stored credentials (future enhancement)
    45→- **Negative**: Key compromise exposes all credentials (mitigated by: key stored in env, not in DB; server access required)
    46→

1→# ADR-067: Non-Streaming Tool Call Interception First
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→Proposed
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→The proxy must intercept `tool_use` blocks in LLM responses to route Brain-managed tool calls. The Anthropic Messages API supports two response modes:
     8→
     9→1. **Non-streaming**: Full JSON response with `content` array containing `tool_use` blocks
    10→2. **Streaming**: SSE events where `content_block_start` (type=tool_use) and `content_block_delta` events must be accumulated to reconstruct tool calls
    11→
    12→Streaming interception is significantly more complex: the proxy must buffer SSE events, detect tool_use block boundaries, accumulate partial JSON from deltas, decide when to pause the stream, execute tools, then either inject results or manage a multi-turn loop -- all while maintaining SSE connection health.
    13→
    14→## Decision
    15→Walking skeleton implements non-streaming tool interception only. The proxy detects Brain-managed tool_use blocks in the full response body, executes them, and constructs a follow-up request with tool_result messages to continue the conversation.
    16→
    17→Streaming requests that contain injected Brain-managed tools are temporarily downgraded to non-streaming for the tool interception loop. Once all tool calls are resolved and the LLM produces a final text response, the result is returned. Alternatively, the walking skeleton can limit tool injection to non-streaming requests only (skip injection when `stream: true`).
    18→
    19→Streaming tool interception is deferred to a later phase.
    20→
    21→## Alternatives Considered
    22→
    23→### Alternative 1: Implement both streaming and non-streaming simultaneously
    24→- **What**: Full SSE event accumulation, partial JSON reconstruction, stream pause/resume.
    25→- **Expected impact**: Complete feature parity from day one.
    26→- **Why rejected**: Doubles implementation complexity for the walking skeleton. The proxy's streaming path (`extractSSEUsage`, `TransformStream`) is already complex. Adding tool call accumulation, execution, and result injection into the SSE pipeline is a separate engineering effort. Walking skeleton principle: prove the vertical slice works first.
    27→
    28→### Alternative 2: Client-side tool execution via response modification
    29→- **What**: Let the LLM response pass through with tool_use blocks, expect the agent runtime to execute them.
    30→- **Expected impact**: Zero proxy-side interception needed.
    31→- **Why rejected**: Agent runtimes don't know about Brain-managed tools. They would encounter unknown tool_use blocks and either error or ignore them. The entire point of proxy-based tool injection is that execution is also proxy-managed -- the agent runtime never handles Brain tools.
    32→
    33→## Consequences
    34→- **Positive**: Walking skeleton ships faster with simpler interception logic
    35→- **Positive**: Full JSON parsing (non-streaming) is straightforward and well-tested
    36→- **Positive**: Multi-turn tool loop is easier to reason about without SSE state management
    37→- **Negative**: Streaming requests with Brain-managed tools may experience degraded UX (either downgraded to non-streaming or tools not injected)
    38→- **Negative**: Streaming tool interception must be implemented later as a separate effort
    39→

1→# ADR-068: Encrypted Field Suffix Convention in Schema
     2→
     3→## Status
     4→Proposed
     5→
     6→## Context
     7→The MCP Tool Registry stores sensitive credential data (OAuth tokens, API keys, passwords) encrypted at rest (ADR-066). The SurrealDB schema needs to represent these fields. The original issue schema (#178) uses plaintext field names (`client_secret`, `access_token`), which creates ambiguity: does the field contain plaintext or ciphertext?
     8→
     9→This ambiguity is dangerous because:
    10→1. A developer might read `client_secret` and assume it's usable directly
    11→2. Query results in admin tools / debugging show the field -- the name should signal "this is ciphertext"
    12→3. Code review cannot distinguish encrypted vs plaintext fields without checking encryption call sites
    13→
    14→## Decision
    15→All encrypted fields use an `_encrypted` suffix: `client_secret_encrypted`, `access_token_encrypted`, `refresh_token_encrypted`, `api_key_encrypted`, `basic_password_encrypted`.
    16→
    17→The plaintext field names (`client_secret`, `access_token`, etc.) do NOT exist in the schema. There is no "plaintext version" stored anywhere.
    18→
    19→Domain types in TypeScript also use the suffix. Decrypted values exist only as local variables within `credential-resolver.ts`, never as persisted or typed domain fields.
    20→
    21→## Alternatives Considered
    22→
    23→### Alternative 1: Same field names as issue schema (no suffix)
    24→- **What**: Use `client_secret`, `access_token` as field names, store ciphertext in them.
    25→- **Expected impact**: Simpler schema, matches the original issue spec.
    26→- **Why rejected**: Ambiguous. A field named `access_token` containing base64 ciphertext is misleading. Every reader of the schema or query result must know the encryption convention. The suffix makes it self-documenting.
    27→
    28→### Alternative 2: Separate encrypted fields table
    29→- **What**: Store all encrypted values in a dedicated `encrypted_field` table with `entity`, `field_name`, `ciphertext` columns.
    30→- **Expected impact**: Centralized encryption, single decryption code path.
    31→- **Why rejected**: Splits the domain model across tables. Credential resolution requires a JOIN for every field. Increases query complexity and latency. The per-field suffix approach keeps the data model cohesive.
    32→
    33→## Consequences
    34→- **Positive**: Self-documenting schema -- any reader knows the field contains ciphertext
    35→- **Positive**: Code review can verify that `_encrypted` fields are only read via the decryption adapter
    36→- **Positive**: Prevents accidental plaintext exposure in query results, admin consoles, backups
    37→- **Negative**: Diverges from the original issue schema field names (documented in data-models.md)
    38→- **Negative**: Slightly longer field names
    39→

Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools.

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

input.command:
gh issue create --title "Streaming tool call interception for proxy tool layer" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

Extend the proxy tool interception layer (step 8.5 from #178) to handle streaming (SSE) responses. The walking skeleton in #178 handles non-streaming tool loops only (ADR-067). This issue covers the streaming path.

## Motivation

When agents send requests with `stream: true`, the proxy currently relays SSE events directly. Once #178 ships, the proxy intercepts tool calls in non-streaming responses — but streaming responses bypass tool interception entirely. Agents using streaming mode won't get Brain-managed tool execution.

## Design Context

From #178's architecture design and ADR-067:

- **Non-streaming** (implemented in #178): Proxy receives full response, parses `content` array for `tool_use` blocks, executes sequentially, returns results as next request's `tool_result` messages
- **Streaming** (this issue): Proxy must accumulate `content_block_start` (type=tool_use) and `content_block_delta` events to reconstruct tool calls, then execute after `message_stop`. SSE relay continues for text blocks.

### Key challenges

1. **Event accumulation**: Tool call arguments arrive across multiple `content_block_delta` events as JSON fragments. Must buffer and reconstruct complete `tool_use` blocks before execution.
2. **Mixed content**: A single streaming response can contain both text blocks (relay immediately) and tool_use blocks (accumulate, execute, then relay results). Need to distinguish and handle both in the same SSE stream.
3. **Span lifetime**: `withTracing()` defers `span.end()` for SSE responses. Tool execution extends the span lifetime — need to ensure trace captures both stream relay and tool execution time.
4. **Multi-turn loop**: After tool execution, results must be sent as a new request to the LLM. The response to that request may also be streaming with more tool calls — the proxy must handle arbitrary-depth streaming tool loops.
5. **Backpressure**: Tool execution (especially integration calls with credential resolution) blocks SSE relay. Client may see a pause in the stream during tool execution.

### Strategy options (from ADR-067)

- **Option A**: Downgrade `stream: true` to `stream: false` when Brain-managed tools are injected. Simplest, but user sees non-streamed response.
- **Option B**: Buffer SSE events, accumulate tool calls, execute, then relay results as synthetic SSE events. Complex but preserves streaming UX.
- **Option C**: Relay text blocks in real-time, pause on tool_use blocks, execute, then resume with tool results. Hybrid approach.

## Depends on

- #178 (MCP tool registry with proxy-based tool injection and credential brokerage)

## References

- `docs/design/mcp-tool-registry/architecture-design.md` § Non-Streaming vs Streaming Tool Call Handling
- `docs/adrs/ADR-067-non-streaming-tool-interception-first.md`
- `app/src/server/proxy/anthropic-proxy-route.ts` (existing SSE relay logic)
- `app/src/server/http/instrumentation.ts` (SSE span lifetime management)
EOF
)" 2>&1
input.description: Create streaming tool interception follow-up issue

content.content: https://github.com/marcus-sa/brain/issues/182

content.text:
Created: **#182** — [Streaming tool call interception for proxy tool layer](https://github.com/marcus-sa/brain/issues/182)

Covers the streaming SSE path deferred by ADR-067, with three strategy options and the key challenges (event accumulation, mixed content, multi-turn loops, backpressure).

data.command:
Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools.

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

content.text:
# NW-DISTILL: Acceptance Test Creation and Business Validation

**Wave**: DISTILL (wave 5 of 6) | **Agent**: Quinn (nw-acceptance-designer)

## Overview

Create E2E acceptance tests from requirements|architecture|infrastructure design using Given-When-Then format. Produces executable specifications bridging business requirements and technical implementation. Infrastructure design from DEVOP informs test environment setup.

## Interactive Decision Points

### Decision 1: Feature Scope
**Question**: What is the scope of this feature?
**Options**:
1. Core feature -- primary application functionality
2. Extension -- modular add-on or integration
3. Bug fix -- regression tests for a known defect

### Decision 2: Test Framework
**Question**: Which test framework to use?
**Options**:
1. pytest-bdd -- Python BDD framework
2. Cucumber -- Ruby/JS BDD framework
3. SpecFlow -- .NET BDD framework
4. Custom -- user provides details

### Decision 3: Integration Approach
**Question**: How should integration tests connect to services?
**Options**:
1. Real services -- test against actual running services
2. Test containers -- ephemeral containers for dependencies
3. Mocks for external only -- real internal, mocked external services

### Decision 4: Infrastructure Testing
**Question**: Should acceptance tests cover infrastructure concerns?
**Options**:
1. Yes -- include CI/CD validation, deployment smoke tests
2. No -- functional acceptance tests only

## Context Files Required

- docs/feature/{feature-name}/discuss/requirements.md | user-stories.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/design/architecture-design.md | component-boundaries.md | technology-stack.md
- docs/feature/{feature-name}/deliver/* (infrastructure design from DEVOP)

## Rigor Profile Integration

Before dispatching the acceptance designer, read rigor config from `.nwave/des-config.json` (key: `rigor`). If absent, use standard defaults.

- **`agent_model`**: Pass as `model` parameter to Task tool. If `"inherit"`, omit `model` (inherits from session).

## Agent Invocation

@nw-acceptance-designer

Execute \*create-acceptance-tests for {feature-name}.

Context files: see above.

**Configuration:**
- model: rigor.agent_model (omit if "inherit")
- test_type: {Decision 1: core|extension|bugfix}
- test_framework: {Decision 2: specflow|cucumber|pytest-bdd}
- integration_approach: {Decision 3} | infrastructure_testing: {Decision 4}
- interactive: moderate | output_format: gherkin

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All user stories have corresponding acceptance tests
- [ ] Step methods call real production services (no mocks at acceptance level)
- [ ] One-at-a-time implementation strategy established (@skip/@pending tags)
- [ ] Tests exercise driving ports, not internal components (hexagonal boundary)
- [ ] Walking skeleton created first with user-centric scenarios (features only; optional for bugs)
- [ ] Infrastructure test scenarios included (if Decision 4 = Yes)
- [ ] Handoff package ready for nw-software-crafter (DELIVER wave)

## Examples

### Example 1: Core feature acceptance tests
```
/nw:distill payment-webhook --test-framework=pytest-bdd --integration=real-services
```
Quinn creates Given-When-Then acceptance tests from requirements and architecture, establishes walking skeleton first, then milestone features with @skip tags for one-at-a-time implementation.

## Next Wave

**Handoff To**: nw-software-crafter (DELIVER wave)
**Deliverables**: Feature files|step definitions|test-scenarios.md|walking-skeleton.md

## Expected Outputs

```
tests/{test-type-path}/{feature-name}/acceptance/
  walking-skeleton.feature
  milestone-{N}-{description}.feature
  integration-checkpoints.feature
  steps/
    conftest.py
    {domain}_steps.py

docs/feature/{feature-name}/distill/
  test-scenarios.md
  walking-skeleton.md
  acceptance-review.md
```

Bug fix regression tests:
```
tests/regression/{component-or-module}/
  bug-{ticket-or-description}.feature
  steps/
    conftest.py
    {domain}_steps.py

tests/unit/{component-or-module}/
  test_{module}_bug_{ticket-or-description}.py
```
No response requested.

Decisions for this project:
1. **Core feature** — primary proxy/registry functionality
2. **Custom** — Bun test with `acceptance-test-kit.ts` (in-process server + isolated SurrealDB, per `docs/agents/testing.md`)
